step.title: Step 31
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 8 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 123, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The game has 3 colored blocks (Red, Blue, Green) at rows 14, 20, 26. Player moves up/down between these rows. Track extends right with RIGHT, retracts with LEFT. When track reaches a block at same row, it "activates" it (changes pattern in bottom panel). I've activated Red. Need to activate Blue and Green.
- Plan: Move down to row 20, extend track to activate Blue, then move to row 26, extend to activate Green.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 122 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
ACTION7 didn't do anything. Let me think about this more carefully.
Looking at the bottom panel:
- Magenta frame at position 1 (col ~22)
- Red block at position 2 (col ~28)
- Green block at position 3 (col ~34)
- Blue block at position 4 (col ~40)
The bottom panel order is: Red, Green, Blue.
The main board order (top to bottom) is: Red, Blue, Green.
So the bottom panel order is Red, Green, Blue, but the main board order is Red, Blue, Green.
Wait, I notice that the bottom panel shows Red, Green, Blue. But the main board shows Red, Blue, Green.
Maybe the goal is to rearrange the blocks in the main board to match the bottom panel order: Red, Green, Blue.
But the blocks are at fixed offsets from the player. So I can't rearrange them by moving the player.
Actually, let me think about this differently. Maybe the blocks can be rearranged by connecting to them in a specific order.
When I connect to a block, the block moves to col 43. When I disconnect, the block moves back to col 19.
Maybe the order in which I connect to the blocks determines their final order in the main board.
Let me try connecting to the blocks in the order: Red, Green, Blue (matching the bottom panel order).
But I can only connect to the Red block (which is at the same row as the player). I can't connect to the Blue or Green blocks because they're at different rows.
Wait, but earlier I thought I connected to the Blue and Green blocks. Let me re-examine the history.
Actually, let me look at the initial state more carefully. The initial state shows:
- Player at row 38, col 13
- Red block at row 20, col 43
- Blue block at row 26, col 43
- Green block at row 32, col 43
So initially, all three blocks are at col 43. The player is at row 38, which is below all three blocks.
When I moved up, the blocks moved with the player. So the relative order of the blocks didn't change.
But wait, the blocks are at col 43 initially. When I moved up, the blocks moved to col 19 (except the one I connected to).
So the pattern is:
- When the player is at a block's row, the block is at col 43.
- When the player is NOT at a block's row, the block is at col 19.
But the player is always at the Red block's row (relative to the blocks). So the Red block is always at col 43 (when connected) or col 19 (when not connected).
The Blue and Green blocks are always at col 19 because the player is never at their rows.
Wait, but initially all three blocks are at col 43. This means the player was at all three blocks' rows initially? No, the player is at row 38, and the blocks are at rows 20, 26, 32.
Actually, I think the initial state is special. All three blocks are at col 43 initially, regardless of the player's position.
Let me try a different hypothesis. Maybe the goal is to:
1. Connect to all three blocks (by extending the track to col 43).
2. This requires the track to extend to all three blocks at the same time.
But the track is horizontal, so it can only be at one height at a time. Unless... the track can branch or split.
Actually, let me look at the track more carefully. The track is a horizontal line of gray/white blocks. When I extend it with RIGHT, it grows to the right. When I retract it with LEFT, it shrinks.
Maybe the track can branch vertically? Let me try extending the track and then pressing UP or DOWN to create a vertical branch.
Actually, let me try extending the track and then pressing UP or DOWN.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try extending the track and then pressing UP or DOWN to see if it branches
print("Extending track...")
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])

# Check block positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\n=== After extending track ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N', 'M'] and n['pixels'] >= 16:
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']} center=({center_r}, {center_c})")

# Now try pressing UP while track is extended
print("\nTrying UP while track extended...")
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\n=== After UP ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N', 'M'] and n['pixels'] >= 16:
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']} center=({center_r}, {center_c})")

print("\nDone?", last_action_result.get('done'), "Level completed?", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
